skin_warnings: B06: composer beat has an empty spark line — SPARK-LINE LAW wants a short serif cue (e.g. "The change,").

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. Anthropic ships a skill with ten color themes. And the most important instruction in it is the one that stops Claude from using them. Before a single hex code lands on your deck, the skill orders Claude to show you a PDF, ask which theme you want — and wait. A styling tool whose first move is to do nothing. Let me show you why that's the smartest line in the file.

props.command: Style this slide deck with the theme-factory skill.
output: Step 1: Show the theme showcase — all ten themes, visually.
Step 2: Ask which theme to apply. Step 3: WAIT for the choice.
Step 4: Only then, apply the colors and fonts.

beats.narration_text: A skill is a folder of instructions Claude reads before it works. This one is tiny. One SKILL.md, about three kilobytes. A themes folder with ten spec files, each around five hundred bytes. One showcase PDF. That's the whole factory. Each theme file is four hex colors, two fonts, and one line about where to use it. A complete design identity that fits in a text message. The size is the first design decision: small enough to read, small enough to trust.

shot.new_visual_element: Skill folder anatomy tree with byte counts

beats.narration_text: Here's one spec sheet in full. Golden Hour. Mustard Yellow, F4A900 — the bold primary accent. Terracotta, C1666B — the warm secondary. Warm Beige, D4B896 for neutral backgrounds. Chocolate Brown, 4A403A for dark text and anchors. Headers in FreeSans Bold, body in FreeSans. Best used for — the file's own words — restaurant presentations, hospitality brands, fall campaigns. Four colors, two fonts, one sentence of intent. That's a theme. Nothing in it is code. It's a brief, written for whoever applies it — and that whoever is Claude.

shot.new_visual_element: Golden Hour museum spec sheet — true hexes

beats.narration_text: Now the thesis. The skill's usage instructions are four steps. Show the showcase. Ask for their choice. Wait for selection. Apply the theme. Look at steps two and three. They automate nothing. They exist to forbid automation. The skill's exact words: 'Get explicit confirmation about the chosen theme.' Claude is fully capable of picking a theme for you — the skill makes it illegal. That's a division of labor written into an instruction file: Claude owns consistency. The human owns taste. Most AI styling tools get this exactly backwards.

shot.new_visual_element: Four-node consent gate — WAIT pulses terracotta

beats.act: Act 4 — same deck, ten skins (the demo)
beats.narration_text: So let's run it. One slide. Same title, same bullets, same little chart. Ten themes, applied live. Ocean Depths — maritime and calm. Sunset Boulevard — warm and loud. Forest Canopy — grounded earth tones. Modern Minimalist — grayscale, nothing to hide behind. Golden Hour, the one you just met. Arctic Frost. Desert Rose. Tech Innovation. Botanical Garden. And Midnight Galaxy — deep purples, built for drama. Watch what never changes: the layout. The words. The chart. A theme touches colors and fonts and nothing else. Same plate, ten different prints — and every hex on screen is straight from the theme files.

shot.new_visual_element: One slide card cycling all ten themes, gallery plaques

beats.narration_text: One more skin. Cream page. Warm ink. A single terracotta accent. Look familiar? You're watching one. This video runs on a fixed theme the same way that slide does — the frame never changes, the content does. The trick you just watched is the trick you're inside.

shot.new_visual_element: Demo card re-skins into the video's own palette; frame highlights

beats.narration_text: But what if none of the ten fit? The skill has an answer, and it's the ask you're seeing now: generate a new theme. Describe what you need — say, a brutalist typography channel, cream paper, warm ink, one terracotta accent — and Claude builds a theme card in the same four-color format.

props.command: None of these fit — generate a theme for a brutalist typography channel: cream paper, warm ink, one terracotta accent.
output: New theme drafted: Brutalist Cream — 4 colors, 2 fonts.
Showing it for review before applying.

beats.narration_text: And here's the result — a brand-new spec sheet, assembled in the skill's own format. But watch the last line of the instructions: after generating the theme, show it for review and verification. Then apply. The gate survives. Even for a theme Claude just invented, Claude still isn't allowed to apply it without your sign-off. The consent gate isn't a feature of the ten presets. It's the skill's actual philosophy.

shot.new_visual_element: Custom theme card assembles; review chip blocks apply

beats.narration_text: Now the honest caveat. The skill's entire safety system for readability is one sentence: 'Ensure proper contrast and readability.' One line, doing very heavy lifting. Run the numbers on the skill's own palettes. Midnight Galaxy, silver text on deep purple: twelve point six to one. Triple-A. Beautiful. Golden Hour, chocolate brown on warm beige: five point three. Passes. But Golden Hour's mustard accent on its own beige background: one point zero five to one. That's not a contrast failure, that's invisibility. The skill hands Claude four colors and one sentence of judgment — and the judgment is where themes go to die. A palette is not a brand system. No spacing scale, no iconography, no contrast math. Ten good starting points — and one very load-bearing sentence.

shot.new_visual_element: Live WCAG contrast meter over true-hex swatch pairs

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Theme-factory is ten palettes and a workflow — and the workflow is the invention. Show, ask, wait, apply: a consent gate that forbids Claude from choosing your look, written directly into the instruction file. Each theme is a five-hundred-byte brief — four hexes, two fonts, one line of intent — applied consistently to any artifact. The escape hatch generates new themes, but the gate survives even there: show for review, then apply. And the caveat is real: contrast governance is one sentence, and the skill's own palettes can fail it. Consistency automated. Taste preserved. Judgment — still yours.

artifactLines: The workflow is the invention: SHOW → ASK → WAIT → APPLY — a consent gate that forbids Claude from choosing your look.
A theme is a ~500-byte brief: four hex colors, two fonts, one line of intent — applied to any artifact, consistently.
The escape hatch generates new themes on demand, but the gate survives: show for review, then apply.
The caveat: contrast governance is one sentence — Golden Hour's own mustard-on-beige runs 1.05:1. Judgment stays human.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here's the prompt — read it with me. 'Here's my deck. Use the theme-factory skill: show me the showcase and let me pick — but before I choose, tell me which theme YOU would have picked for this content, and what that choice says about my audience.' That second clause is the fun part. The skill forbids Claude from choosing — this prompt makes it show you the choice it wasn't allowed to make. You get the consent gate AND the machine's opinion, in that order. Run it on a real deck, and check whether Claude's pick matches yours. The gap between them is your brand talking. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Here's my deck. Use the theme-factory skill: show me the showcase and let me pick — but before I choose, tell me which theme YOU would have picked for this content, and what that choice says about my audience.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Restrained. Liam, in for Bear.
